text.title: PM Tool Selection Process
text.summary: MoSCoW requirements gathering, hard-constraint pruning, weighted scoring, and ADR output format for defensible tool selection.

rule.statement: Every Must-Have requirement must have a stakeholder quote or measured pain point as evidence; requirements without evidence are downgraded to Should-Have.
rule.rationale: Evidence-anchored MoSCoW prevents post-selection disputes ("I said we needed X"). It also forces specificity that reveals when requirements conflict.

rule.statement: Apply hard constraints first (price ceiling, data residency, compliance certification, IDP/SCIM support) to reduce the universe to 3-5 candidates before evaluating features.
rule.rationale: Compliance posture (SOC2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, FedRAMP, GDPR data residency) can flip the entire shortlist; evaluating features before checking compliance wastes effort.

rule.statement: Time-box the POC to two weeks using actual project data, not sample data; longer evaluation causes decision fatigue without proportional insight.
rule.rationale: Real data surfaces integration gaps, performance issues, and UX friction that demo data hides. Two weeks is sufficient for most team-size decisions.

rule.statement: Include "do nothing" as a baseline option in the ADR with quantified cost of staying on the current tool.
rule.rationale: Without the baseline, the recommendation reads as "we must switch" rather than "switching is better than staying by X%."

antipattern.description: POC driven only by API scenarios without any UI testing.
antipattern.reason: API success does not predict UI adoption. One Playwright test per shortlisted vendor covering the most common UI flow catches issues that break real adoption.

rule.statement: Score with weights and run sensitivity analysis; if the winner changes under plus-or-minus 20% weight perturbations, the choice is fragile and requires deeper investigation.
rule.rationale: A fragile winner means requirements are genuinely contested. Surface this before commitment rather than discovering it post-implementation.

rule.statement: Quantify migration cost (data ETL, automations, integrations, training, parallel-run period) in the ADR — typically 30-50% of year-1 TCO.
rule.rationale: Migration cost is the most underestimated factor in tool selection and the most common source of post-decision regret.

example.code:
# ADR: PM Tool Selection — [Date]
## Decision Adopt Linear for the engineering org (Jira remains for compliance projects).
## Context Team of 45 engineers; GitHub-first; previous Jira instance at 180 custom fields.
## Requirements (MoSCoW) ### Must - GitHub PR linking (evidence: @eng-lead, transcript offset 142) - Sub-$15/user/month (evidence: budget doc Q3)
## Scoring (1-5 scale, weighted) | Criterion | Weight | Linear | Jira | ClickUp | |-----------|--------|--------|------|---------| | UX speed | 0.30 | 5 | 2 | 3 | | GitHub | 0.25 | 5 | 4 | 3 | | Reporting | 0.20 | 2 | 5 | 4 | | Price | 0.25 | 4 | 3 | 4 | | **Total** | | **4.1**| **3.4** | **3.5** |
## Sensitivity: ranking stable under ±20% weight perturbation.
## Migration cost estimate Data: 12,000 issues. Automations: 34 rules. Training: 45 people. Parallel run: 4 weeks. Total: ~$28k one-time.

text.reference: MoSCoW requirements document template with stakeholder interview questions
POC scenario harness for driving scripted tests against REST-enabled PM tools
